content.rendered:
It sounds like a Chuck Berry song and reads like a Bruce Springsteen song.

With lyrics about Cadillacs roaring through Nebraska, running from state troopers and driving all night, “Get Out of Denver” sounds like it foretells more of Springsteen’s catalog than Bob Seger’s.

Seger released “Get Out of Denver” in 1974, way back when the E Street Band (which hadn’t even been named yet) wove Berry’s classics into their sets on a nightly basis. It was never even remotely a hit. It barely cracked the Billboard Hot 100, and the album it led off didn’t even make the Billboard Top 200.

But Bob Seger’s “Get Out of Denver” has remarkable staying power nevertheless, and today both the song and Seger’s 1974 Seven album are recognized as classics. As the first song on the album, “Get Out of Denver” has the distinction of being the first song many fans ever heard by Bob Seger and The Silver Bullet Band (Seger had six previous albums, but Seven was the debut of his now iconic band.)

Strangely, though, despite Bruce’s friendship with and appreciation for Seger (the two performed together in 1980), Bruce didn’t start covering “Get Out of Denver” until the new century–first at a pair of benefit shows for his kids’ school in 2002 (for which there are no circulating recordings), later at a pair of shows (a decade apart) in the titular city.

Here’s the first of those on-location performances, the show-opener when Bruce’s Rising Tour stopped in Denver on September 25, 2003.

Almost a decade later, Bruce opened another Denver show with “Get Out of Denver”–this time on the Wrecking Ball Tour.

“Get Out of Denver” makes for a great opener, and with its “Little Queenie”-ish riff and chorus, it would make for a great encore, too. Let’s hope Bruce brings this one out more often.

Get Out of Denver
First performed: April 13, 2002 (Asbury Park, NJ)
Last performed: November 19, 2012 (Denver, CO)

© July 11, 2019

excerpt.rendered: Catch Bruce playing in Denver, and you just might get lucky enough to hear him cover Bob Seger’s 1974 classic.

jetpack-related-posts.title: Cover Me: Ramblin’ Gamblin’ Man
jetpack-related-posts.author: Ken
jetpack-related-posts.excerpt: Two bands, one venue, and one great tribute: watch Bruce Springsteen cover Bob Seger's first great hit.

jetpack-related-posts.title: September 2
jetpack-related-posts.author: Ken
jetpack-related-posts.excerpt: The Human Rights Now! Tour kicks off, Bruce and Bob Seger meet for the first time, Bruce and the E Street Band help inaugurate the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame, and more.

jetpack-related-posts.title: September 25
jetpack-related-posts.author: Ken
jetpack-related-posts.excerpt: On this date: the release of Magic, guest appearances with U2 and Eddie Vedder, and a spectacular night with Elvis Costello. More highlights inside.

jetpack-related-posts.title: Cover Me: Purple Rain
jetpack-related-posts.author: Ken
jetpack-related-posts.excerpt: Two days after Prince's untimely departure, Bruce and the E Street Band took the stage in purple and delivered one of their greatest covers ever.

jetpack-related-posts.title: Cover Me, Ghost Hounds and Sasha Allen: Thunder Road
jetpack-related-posts.author: Ken
jetpack-related-posts.excerpt: Pittsburgh rockers The Ghost Hounds team up with Sasha Allen on a terrific cover of one of Bruce's greatest hits.

jetpack-related-posts.title: October 20
jetpack-related-posts.author: Ken
jetpack-related-posts.excerpt: Bruce's final show at the Spectrum, a guest appearance with Neil Young and Bob Dylan, a hometown Houserockers show, those famous Time and Newsweek covers, and more--all from this date.
